title: Relative rank and regularity
speaker: Tamar Ziegler
affiliation: Hebrew University; Distinguished Visiting Professor, School of Mathematics
abstract: The notion of Schmidt rank/strength for a collection of m polynomials plays an important role in additive combinatorics, number theory and commutative algebra; high rank collections of polynomials are “psuedorandom”.  An arbitrary collection of polynomials is not necessarily of high rank, but via a regularity procedure is contained in an ideal generated by a huge (depending on m) high rank collection of polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Schmidt rank/strength and high-rank counting
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Schmidt rank, also called strength, for a polynomial and for a collection, and recalls Schmidt’s theorem that sufficiently high rational rank yields an asymptotic count for common integer zeros.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field bias-rank and equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: In the higher-order Fourier/additive-combinatorics setting, the speaker explains measurable rank, its equivalence to Schmidt rank in large characteristic, and the bias-rank theorem that high rank forces joint equidistribution of polynomial level sets independently of the number of variables.

proof_stages.label: Strength, Stillman’s theorem, and regular sequences
proof_stages.summary: A commutative-algebra motivation is reviewed: strength was used in the proof of Stillman’s conjecture because high-rank collections behave like independent variables and form regular sequences with controlled projective resolutions.

proof_stages.label: Classical regularization and blow-up
proof_stages.summary: The speaker identifies the obstruction that arbitrary polynomial collections are not high rank and describes the standard regularization process of replacing a low-rank linear combination by lower-degree factors, which terminates but causes a rapidly growing number of polynomials.

proof_stages.label: Relative rank for subsets and filtered systems
proof_stages.summary: To keep the benefits of high rank while making regularization cheaper, the talk defines the rank of a polynomial relative to a subset and sketches the extension to filtered collections, where each layer is measured on the locus cut out by previous layers.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-size relative regularization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that over finite fields of characteristic larger than the degree, any bounded-degree collection can be enlarged to only polynomially many generators whose ideal contains the original collection and which are jointly equidistributed via a relative bias-rank theorem intrinsic to the lower variety.

proof_stages.label: Scope and consequences of the relative theory
proof_stages.summary: The final discussion explains that the relative notion retains the desired consequences, such as regular sequences over algebraically closed fields and useful finite-field equidistribution on bounded-codimension subvarieties, while adapting Schmidt-type counting remains work in progress.

examples.name: Rank-one decomposable polynomial
examples.note: The speaker notes that a rank-one polynomial is simply decomposable, making the Schmidt-rank definition concrete without driving the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only says that a rank-one polynomial is decomposable as an illustration of the definition, without doing any calculation or construction with it

examples.name: Two quadratic forms over a finite field
examples.note: The correlation of two quadratic exponential phases is controlled by the rank of their difference, which is presented as the first visible reason that rank and bias-rank notions are needed.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sets up the exponential sum for two quadratic forms and evaluates its correlation in terms of the rank of their difference

passes.reason: A pair of quadratic forms is a typical finite-field example deployed to show higher-degree exponential polynomials need not be orthogonal.

examples.name: Generic complex polynomial of high rank
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions that a generic polynomial over the complex numbers has rank growing with the number of variables, illustrating that high-rank forms are abundant generically.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only cites the generic complex high-rank behavior and a rough known formula in response to a question, without deriving or applying it
